analysis:
- Algorithmic families: top scorers use either guided stochastic search (TPOSS, Heuristic 1) or exact greedy+local improvement (GSI_LS, Heuristic 2). Middle methods use exact greedy HV-contribution (GHSS) or Monte‑Carlo contribution estimates (Heuristic 4). Lower ones are heavy, multi-component metaheuristics (GL_HSS) or tensor/weight approximations (GAHSS) that trade clarity for complexity.
- Strength vs cost tradeoffs:
  - Exact HV-evaluations (Heuristics 2–3) give high-quality moves but pay huge runtime: they recompute full hypervolumes inside tight loops (per candidate removal or per candidate-per-iteration), causing O(k·n) expensive pg.hypervolume calls.
  - TPOSS (1) reduces search breadth via a small evolving population and targeted mutation; it limits HV calls but still creates/destroys solution arrays and calls HV frequently. It balances exploration and budgeted evaluations.
  - Monte‑Carlo estimator (4) shifts cost into a single precomputation (sample × candidate dominated matrix) and then uses fast vectorized counting—this is much cheaper per-iteration and scales well, at the cost of approximation error and memory for samples.
  - Tensor/weight approximations (6) approximate HVC cheaply via linear algebra, but risk bias and need careful normalization/caching.
- Data reuse and avoidable overhead:
  - Recomputing the same hypervolume or rebuilding pg.hypervolume objects in inner loops (GHSS, GSI_LS) is unnecessary; cache HV results and incremental changes (remove/add) where possible.
  - Dominance tests can be vectorized and reused (as in Heuristic 4). Precompute dominance relations, non-dominated filtering, and pairwise contributions to avoid repeated expensive HV calls.
  - Heavy recursive generation and many prints/logs (GL_HSS) add overhead and code complexity without corresponding benefit in constructive phases.
  - Many heuristics rebuild arrays/convert types inside loops; using preallocated numpy arrays and boolean masks speeds tight loops.
- Practical structure recommendations derived from comparisons:
  - Prefilter dominated candidates and limit the candidate pool before costly evaluation (helps all methods).
  - Use a hybrid: cheap approximate contribution (Monte‑Carlo or tensor proxy) to shortlist ~O(k) candidates, then evaluate exact HV for those with cached/incremental updates, and apply local swap improvements.
  - Keep the number of exact HV calls linear in k (not in n·k) by caching and incremental computation, or by restricting exact evaluations to a small candidate set.
experience: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.
current_reflection: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: hybrid constructive, dominance filtering, vectorized Monte‑Carlo, tensor proxy, shortlist, cached/incremental hypervolume, swap local search, batch processing.
- Advice: pipeline: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized MC or fast tensor proxies to shortlist, run exact hypervolume only on shortlist using cached/incremental updates, then run a small swap-based local search; batch and vectorize heavy work.
- Avoid: rebuilding objects in loops, per-candidate full exact HV, unvectorized sampling, recomputing dominance repeatedly.
- Explanation: narrows costly exact HV to few candidates, leverages SIMD/tensor speed and caching to cut computation, and uses lightweight local search to improve solution quality efficiently.
